title: ‘Summer House’ and ‘Survivor’ Should Be Locks for Emmys

blocks.content: If the Emmys are good for anything, it’s providing a snapshot of the TV landscape for that given year. Seinfeld’s rise to America’s elite sitcom coincided with its Emmy victory for Outstanding Comedy Series in 1993. The debuts of Sex and the City and The Sopranos in 1998–99 were met with a slew of major nominations. Schitt’s Creek’s and Ted Lasso’s Emmy wins remain a snapshot of COVID lockdown and a rush toward comfort comedies. The Emmys don’t always achieve this degree of finger-on-the-pulse accuracy, but we forever hope they can get there.

This year, two shows have dominated television in terms of headlines and conversation: Summer House, with its juicy sex-and-betrayal scandal, and Survivor’s landmark 50th season. Emmy history isn’t in either show’s favor; Summer House has received zero nominations in Outstanding Unstructured Reality category across its first nine seasons, while Survivor’s status as the Ur-text of modern reality TV hasn’t translated to a single win in the Outstanding Reality Competition category. The reality categories are notoriously repetitive when it comes to nominees and winners year to year, so the signature shows of 2026 are far from guaranteed their rewards.

If you’re wondering how Survivor never won an Emmy despite inventing the reality-strategy series as we know it, the answer is that it did — just not in the Outstanding Reality Competition category we know today. That category didn’t come into being until 2003. Back in 2001, the first year Survivor was Emmy eligible, it won in the Outstanding Non-Fiction Program (Special Class) category, beating out an eclectic mix of shows including TLC staple Junkyard Wars, MTV’s Road Rules, the underrated one-season-VH1-wonder Bands on the Run, and the other Mark Burnett–produced outdoor-adventure series, Eco-Challenge: Borneo. Two years later, the Emmys instituted the Reality Competition category, but by this time, Survivor was on its fifth and sixth seasons, and ratings behemoth American Idol seemed primed to take the win. Instead, the voters made the surprise choice to anoint The Amazing Race, then famously stuck with that choice for nine of the next ten years. Survivor was a perpetual nominee until 2006, then wouldn’t be nominated again until 2023 and 2025.

That Survivor fell out of favor with Emmy voters around 2006 is not surprising. The show experienced a bit of a lull following its 2004 all-star season, hit an all-time peak in 2010 (the year of Heroes vs. Villains), then found its stride around the mid-2010s. The Emmys, however, don’t recognize reality shows as having peaks and valleys, with some seasons deserving of wins and others that shouldn’t be nominated at all. Instead, voters play favorites until a show falls off, then ignore it forever. Project Runway was nominated every year from 2005 until 2018, then never again. Some of that show’s earliest seasons represented reality TV at its apex at that time; Zulema and the motherfucking walk-off? Christian Siriano’s ascent? Come on! One of those seasons ought to have beat The Amazing Race for one of its ten wins. Project Runway never won once. By that same token, the show dipped in quality and cultural relevance long before 2018, yet was a reliable rubber-stamp nominee.

This year’s roster of predicted nominees for Outstanding Reality Competition don’t offer much hope for change. RuPaul’s Drag Race hasn’t missed a nomination since 2017, its ninth season, and even the show’s most ardent fans would admit there have been great seasons and subpar seasons in the decade since. The Voice was nominated every year from 2012 through 2024, only knocked off last year by the one-two punch of newcomer The Traitors and the somewhat unexpected nod for Survivor. Survivor’s return nomination in 2023 after a 17-year absence was the first time a show returned to the Reality Competition nominee list after having dropped out for any length of time. It was the first glimmer of hope that Emmy voters might be willing to react to fluctuations in the reality landscape: Survivor’s surge of popularity after the COVID lockdown (when, anecdotally, everybody you know binged the series) deserved to be reflected in the Emmy voting. Sources close to the show are not shy about touting its awards case for 2026, noting the series’ “decades of innovation and cultural impact,” and underlining the fact that a vote for Survivor 50 recognizes “not just a single season, but a foundational franchise that shaped modern television.”

Getting back into the nomination ring two of the last three years makes Survivor a likely nominee this year — but can it win? If this were the Oscars, the biggest Emmy narrative by far would be that, in its 50th season, Survivor is 23 years overdue for an Emmy win. It is the Paul Thomas Anderson of this year’s Emmys. The Leonardo DiCaprio in The Revenant. The Susan Lucci at the Daytime Emmys in 1999. The question is whether Emmy voters can be dissuaded from voting for their new fave, an admittedly excellent fourth season of The Traitors.

The situation Summer House faces is even more daunting, considering the show has been completely snubbed in the Emmy department over its first nine seasons. However, such was the case for Vanderpump Rules during the Scandoval season. Before that, no Real Housewives iteration or spinoff (Vanderpump spawned from the literal end of a Real Housewives of Beverly Hills episode, and Summer House then launched with a backdoor pilot on Vanderpump) had ever been nominated in the Outstanding Unstructured Reality category, not even during their buzziest seasons (the year of RHONY’s “Scary Island,” the Emmy winner was Jamie Oliver’s Food Revolution; the year Kim Richards called Eileen Davidson a “beast” in Amsterdam, the Emmy winner was Deadliest Catch). But when the news broke in March 2023 that Tom Sandoval had cheated on his longtime girlfriend, Ariana Madix with co-star Raquel Leviss and was leaving Ariana to be with Raquel, the news broke free of the Bravo bubble and became a major pop-culture event. At the Emmy nominations that July, Vanderpump Rules achieved the impossible and scored an Emmy nomination in 2023, and followed with a second nod in 2024.

If that’s what it takes to get a Housewives-style reality series nominated, wouldn’t it follow that Summer House’s currently unfolding scandal could see a similar boost? Just as with Scandoval, the revelation that a recently separated Amanda Batula and an undeniably beguiling West Wilson are now an item (What kind of item? They haven’t articulated!), betraying Ciara Miller and (I suppose if we must) Kyle Cooke in the process, has set every facet of the Bravo universe ablaze. Cast members and A-listers are proudly Team Ciara; every single social-media platform is all kinds of worked up; and parasocial dividing lines have been trampled into dust. With the reunion episodes concluding two nights before Emmy-nomination voting begins on June 11, there’s a good chance that the TV show many voters will have at the front of their minds when they cast those ballots will be Summer House.

Other than the Scandoval blip, Bravo’s house style — specifically the Housewives-y lifestyle series — has never been particularly favored by the Emmys. The few Bravo shows that have seen awards success have been competition series like Top Chef and early Project Runway or shows with inclusivity messages like Queer Eye for the Straight Guy and Kathy Griffin: My Life on the D-List (the latter’s success can likely be attributed to Griffin’s nonstop hustling on her own behalf). In the 2020s, the only Bravo franchise to get any real traction in the Unstructured Reality category has been Below Deck, which has seen one nomination apiece for its original iteration (2021), its Mediterranean spinoff (2022), and its Down Under version (2024). Perhaps the Emmy voters are handing out degree-of-difficulty points for filming on the high seas instead of a backyard in Montauk.

Moreover, in this age of entertainment conglomerates, internal competition matters, and Summer House has a ton of company underneath the great NBC Universal umbrella. Bravo currently lists four shows under the Unstructured Reality heading on its FYC site: Below Deck: Mediterranean, Married to Medicine, The Real Housewives of Salt Lake City, and Summer House. It’s definitely a good sign that Summer House is on that list — the same can’t be said for shows like The Real Housewives of Beverly Hills or the rebooted Vanderpump Rules — but while Below Deck and RHOSLC are having FYC events in L.A., Summer House is absent from the Emmys’ FYC event calendar. Then there’s the Critics Choice Real TV Awards, which nominated Summer House for Best Ensemble Cast and Ciara Miller for Female Star of the Year — two categories that don’t have equivalents at the Emmys (though they should) — yet didn’t nominate the show for Best Unstructured Series despite nominating three Bravo shows: Below Deck: Mediterranean, The Real Housewives of Salt Lake City, and The Real Housewives of Rhode Island. If Summer House can’t win the battle for attention within its home base of Bravo, an Emmy nomination is going to be a tall order.
